question.text: Premium paid on commodity options is accounted for as:

answer.text: An expense in the period it relates to — recognized as the cost of acquiring the right
A capital expenditure to be depreciated over the option's life
Not recorded until the option is exercised
Added to the cost of the underlying commodity

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Option premium is an expense (for buyers) in the period it relates to. If the option expires worthless, the entire premium is expensed. If exercised, it becomes part of the acquisition/disposal cost. **The Trap:** Not capital expenditure. Must be recorded when paid (not at exercise). Not directly added to commodity cost until exercise. **Cross-Exam Context:** Option accounting applies across all derivative markets. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** For tax purposes, expired option premiums are typically deductible as business expenses — maintain proper records of all option transactions for tax filing.
